sources.title: Supporting Secondary Teachers’ Critical Disciplinary Literacies

Climate Change and Biology Socratic Seminar

messages.content:
To support secondary students in developing **Critical Disciplinary Literacy (CDL)**, the text outlines a framework that moves beyond traditional disciplinary literacy to include justice-oriented instruction. Below is a summary of the core concepts and implementation strategies provided:

### 1. Conceptual Framework
*   **Disciplinary Literacy vs. CDL:** Conventional disciplinary literacy teaches students to "think like" a field expert. CDL expands on this by questioning the power structures and exclusionary norms inherent in those fields.
*   **Core Objective:** CDL helps students "read the word and the world," allowing them to engage with academic standards while simultaneously analyzing and disrupting systems of inequity and power (p. 5, 6, 7).

### 2. Pedagogy and Strategy Design
The textbook emphasizes that justice-oriented instruction should not be isolated from standard curriculum. Strategies are:
*   **Multi-disciplinary & Accessible:** They provide practical, ready-to-implement methods for ELA, Math, Science, and Social Studies (p. 5).
*   **Standard-Aligned:** Lessons are structured around national standards (e.g., Common Core, NGSS) paired with one of four critical literacy dimensions:
    *   Disrupting the commonplace.
    *   Interrogating multiple viewpoints.
    *   Focusing on sociopolitical issues.
    *   Taking action and promoting social justice (p. 9).

### 3. Implementation Process
The educator-led process for developing these lessons follows a scaffolded approach:
*   **Student-Centered Creation:** Teacher candidates (pre-service teachers) learn to select scholarly texts, design collaborative activities (e.g., Fishbowl, Socratic Seminars, Poster Carousels), and create reflection materials (p. 8-9).
*   **Expert Integration:** The model encourages inviting expert practitioners and providing repeated opportunities for drafting and peer-feedback to ensure lessons are both conceptually sound and physically executable in a classroom (p. 8, 9).

### 4. Goal for Educators
The overarching goal is to prepare both pre-service and in-service teachers to foster student agency. By using CDL strategies, educators honor students’ "funds of knowledge," increase classroom engagement, and support learners in navigating the sociopolitical realities of the world around them (p. 6, 7, 10).

Lesson 3: Disciplinary Literacy - Presentation16 slides
Lesson 3: Disciplinary Literacy
  • Understanding content reading and writing strategies.
  • Defining Critical Disciplinary Literacy (CDL).
  • Integrating standards with social justice.

This text, titled *Supporting Secondary Teachers’ Critical Disciplinary Literacies*, serves as a resource for educators to blend disciplinary literacy with justice-oriented instruction [S.5].

### Core Philosophy
The text defines Critical Disciplinary Literacy (CDL) as a framework that goes beyond teaching students to "think like" a disciplinary expert. Instead, it challenges the idea that academic knowledge is neutral. By utilizing CDL, teachers help students "read the word and the world," encouraging them to analyze how power structures and inequities function within specific disciplines [S.6].

### Key Objectives
The book aims to bridge the gap between abstract pedagogical theory and classroom practice by:
1. Helping pre-service teachers implement research-based CDL strategies [S.7].
2. Empowering educators to address social justice while meeting national standards [S.5].
3. Providing a multimodal, accessible guide for both pre-service and in-service teachers [S.7].

### Methodology and Structure
Authored by Dr. Jeanne Dyches in collaboration with her teacher candidates at Iowa State University, the book was developed through a 16-week scaffolded process involving guest experts and peer feedback [S.8]. Each chapter follows a consistent structure:
* Alignment with disciplinary standards (e.g., Common Core, NGSS) [S.9].
* Application of one or more critical literacy dimensions (disrupting the commonplace, interrogating viewpoints, focusing on sociopolitical issues, or taking action) [S.9].
* Step-by-step implementation guides and video demonstrations [S.9].

Ultimately, the text seeks to support educators in fostering student agency and engagement by teaching them to recognize and disrupt oppression through their disciplinary studies [S.10].

This lesson plan, designed by Ridwan Abdi for a Mathematics Education curriculum, integrates the study of residential segregation with a core mathematics skill: converting fractions and decimals to percentages [S.11-13].

**Lesson Overview and Objectives**
The instruction spans two days and focuses on sociopolitical awareness alongside mathematical proficiency (Common Core 6.NS.C.6.C). The primary goal is to examine how individual biases contribute to systemic residential segregation through the "Parable of the Polygons" simulation [S.11-12].

**Instructional Sequence**
1. Introduction: Students use a KWL-CL (Know, Want to Know, Learned, Connections, Learning strategies) chart to brainstorm factors and consequences of residential segregation [S.11].
2. Investigation: Groups read the article “Attacking the Black–White Opportunity Gap That Comes from Residential Segregation” and engage with the "Parable of the Polygons" game to analyze how micro-biases lead to macro-segregation [S.12].
3. Mathematical Integration: Students identify the presence of mathematics within the reading and game. The instructor uses Google Slides to formalize the connection between the simulation and numerical data [S.12].
4. Assessment and Reflection: Students complete their KWL-CL charts and write a reflection justifying the relationship between fractions and percentages ($ \text{Percentage} = \text{Fraction} \times 100 $) as it relates to the societal trends analyzed [S.12].

**Author Perspective**
The author is a Mathematics Education major from Kenya who emphasizes the transformative power of education. The lesson design reflects a commitment to social impact by helping students reason through how small, individual biases can push entire societies toward inequitable outcomes [S.12-13].

This document outlines a Socratic Seminar lesson plan designed by Samantha Andersen, focusing on the biological and environmental impacts of climate change (p. [S.14]).

**Key Components of the Lesson**

*   **Learning Objectives:** The lesson aligns with the disciplinary literacy standard HS-LS2-7, requiring students to design and evaluate solutions for reducing human impact on the environment while practicing critical thinking regarding multiple viewpoints (p. [S.14]).
*   **Preparation:** Students are provided with two primary texts—a NASA overview of climate change (Dunbar, 2015) and an interactive map of climate risks (Thompson, 2020)—and must complete a graphic organizer beforehand to develop their responses for the seminar (p. [S.14], [S.15]).
*   **Seminar Structure:** The classroom is organized into a circle to facilitate student-led discussion. The teacher acts as a facilitator, ensuring all students participate and that quieter voices are heard (p. [S.15]).
*   **Discussion Approach:** Students analyze data from the climate risk map and address open-ended, complex questions about local impacts, community action, and the distribution of climate risks. The goal is to avoid searching for a single "correct" answer and instead foster creative thinking and the evaluation of multiple scientific perspectives (p. [S.15], [S.16]).
*   **Reflective Closure:** Students are required to take notes on major points during the collaboration, engage in a final reflection, and submit their graphic organizers as an exit pass demonstrating their participation (p. [S.16]).

This lesson plan, designed by Erin Brandt, outlines an instructional strategy centered on interpretive communication and critical literacy using a quote from Adolf Hitler’s *Mein Kampf* [S.18].

Key Points:

The lesson begins with students analyzing the statement: “I know that fewer people are won over by the written word than by the spoken word and that every great movement on this earth owes its growth to the great speakers and not to great writers” [S.18].

The instructional process follows a structured, scaffolded approach:
1. Students reflect on and write down their individual opinions regarding the power of speech versus writing [S.18].
2. Small groups discuss their perspectives, considering personal examples of how arguments are shaped by speech or text [S.19].
3. The class divides into groups representing opposing viewpoints, encouraged to debate and potentially switch sides based on the strength of their peers' arguments [S.19].
4. Finally, the source of the quote is revealed. Students then analyze how this specific ideology was utilized by Hitler, discussing the historical impact on the Nazi regime and the victims of that power [S.19].

The pedagogical goal is to help students practice interpretive communication by deriving meaning through local, personal, and historical contexts. By interrogating multiple viewpoints—including their own, their peers', and those of historical figures—students evaluate the real-world consequences of rhetoric and ideology [S.19].

The provided document outlines two specialized instructional strategies designed for secondary education classrooms.

**Examing Poverty in Latin America (Pages S.21–S.23)**
Mackenzie Crampton’s lesson for Spanish 4 students utilizes a "Poster Carousel" to explore sociopolitical issues. Key components include:
Reading and discussing an article on poverty rates in Latin America and the impact of COVID-19. 
Dividing students into groups to rotate through four questions placed on different corners of a poster. 
Mandating that all responses and discussions be conducted in Spanish to improve verbal and written communication. 
Engaging in a class-wide reflection where students analyze the information and present their perspectives on the subject.

**Cornell Notes and the "Explained" Series (Pages S.24–S.26)**
Will Douglas’s approach for English students centers on analyzing the central idea of a documentary episode. Key components include:
Utilizing the "Music" episode of Vox’s Explained to investigate the philosophical inquiry of "what makes us human." 
Using pre-structured Cornell notes that focus on five musical elements: rhythm, pitch, harmony, melody, and timbre. 
Guiding students to compare and contrast human musical capabilities with those of other animal species. 
Facilitating a terminal discussion that encourages students to evaluate the definition of humanity and the significance of biological traits through open-ended questions.

This document outlines two instructional activities designed to foster critical thinking and student engagement in Secondary Education classrooms.

**Analyzing Pascal’s Triangle (pp. 23-25)**
Sullivan Fitzgerald’s lesson utilizes the RAFT (Role, Audience, Format, Topic) strategy to explore the history of Pascal’s Triangle. Students are assigned specific roles to research various mathematicians associated with the triangle’s origin. The goal is to evaluate historical claims and address the marginalization of certain contributors. The activity involves short video introductions, small-group research and debate, and a final class-wide reflection to analyze who deserves historical credit. The disciplinary focus includes interpreting mathematical expressions, such as $A-SSE.A.1$, which involves viewing parts of an expression as a single entity.

**Gender Inequality Fishbowl Discussion (pp. 26-27)**
Kayla Gerard presents a lesson focused on sociopolitical literacy using a Fishbowl Discussion format. Students are divided into two groups, each assigned a specific article regarding gender inequality. Following individual reading and small-group preparation, the class engages in a structured dialogue. One group occupies an inner circle to discuss guiding questions while an outer group listens, later posing follow-up questions to the participants. The groups then switch roles. This method aims to improve reading comprehension and critical thinking, culminating in an exit ticket where students reflect on the sociopolitical themes presented in the texts.

This section outlines two distinct pedagogical approaches for secondary classrooms: a literacy-based strategy for ELA and a collaborative research method for History.

**Annotating Informational Text (Selma Hasan, pp. 32-35)**
This lesson plan focuses on CCSS.ELA-LITERACY.RI.9-10.4, emphasizing the analysis of word choice, tone, and sociopolitical viewpoints. Students engage in close reading through a structured annotation process:
$1.$ Underlining major information.
$2.$ Highlighting difficult vocabulary.
$3.$ Circling keywords.
$4.$ Documenting personal reactions.
$5.$ Connecting themes between two diverse texts (a Vox article and an excerpt by Ibram X. Kendi).
The strategy is designed to foster critical thinking and active engagement with complex social justice topics.

**The Great Depression Jigsaw Method (Carter Junge, pp. 36-38)**
This lesson addresses social studies standards regarding governmental responses to the Great Depression. The instructional model follows these steps:
$1.$ Initial reading of Eric Rauchway’s text to identify core social programs implemented during the economic crisis.
$2.$ Whole-group discussion evaluating the importance of these programs.
$3.$ A Jigsaw activity where four groups analyze specific initiatives: the Agricultural Adjustment Act (AAA), the Civilian Conservation Corps (CCC), the Works Progress Administration (WPA), and the Civil Works Administration (CWA).
$4.$ Peer-to-peer education where groups share their findings, supported by textual evidence, to ensure all students understand each program's impact on the government and the populace. 

Both authors, Selma Hasan and Carter Junge, emphasize the importance of moving beyond rote note-taking to build deep, evidence-based understandings and personal connections to the subject matter.

This document presents three distinct instructional lesson plans focused on disciplinary and critical literacy, as developed by education students.

**Addressing the Effects of Minimum Wage (pp. [S.39]–[S.41])**
Designed by Juliana Lobraco, this math-focused lesson utilizes quantitative analysis to explore the sociopolitical impact of minimum wage. Students use real-world data from the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics to identify independent and dependent variables, plot wage growth, and create linear equations ($y = mx + b$). By comparing different occupations against the state minimum wage, students analyze income inequality and the relationship between hourly earnings and the cost of living.

**Using the Fishbowl Strategy to Discuss Book Banning (pp. [S.42]–[S.43])**
Created by Ainsley Lovrien, this English lesson examines the practice of book censorship. Students are divided into "pro" and "con" groups to analyze articles regarding book banning before participating in a fishbowl discussion. This activity encourages critical thinking about sociopolitical issues, such as how censorship targets literature related to race, slavery, and political identity.

**Langston Hughes and Walt Whitman; Venn Diagrams (pp. [S.44]–[S.45])**
Prepared by Ashley Luedtke, this literature lesson uses Venn diagrams to compare Walt Whitman’s "I Hear America Singing" and Langston Hughes’ "I, Too, Sing America." By employing close reading skills, students analyze the socioeconomic and historical contexts of each poet. The lesson concludes by asking students to apply these findings through a modern lens, determining how the themes of pride and social status would manifest in contemporary society.

This document outlines three distinct pedagogical approaches designed to foster disciplinary and critical literacy in secondary education students.

**1. TPCASTT+CL for World War I Poetry (pp. [S.46]–[S.50])**
James Mechikoff presents a guided close-reading framework, "TPCASTT+CL," to analyze Jessie Pope's *War Girls*. This method incorporates Title, Paraphrase, Connotation, Attitude, Shifts, Title (reevaluation), Theme, and Critical Literacy. By focusing on a female poet during WWI, the lesson disrupts the commonplace focus on male-authored war poetry, bringing visibility to marginalized perspectives.

**2. Response Heuristic for Imagery (pp. [S.51]–[S.53])**
Crystal Millan-Herrera details a strategy for analyzing Maya Angelou’s *Still I Rise*. Using a three-column "Response Heuristic Graphic Organizer," students identify instances of imagery, explain the underlying figurative language, and establish personal or sociocultural connections. This lesson emphasizes interrogating multiple viewpoints, validating student identity, and exploring the experiences of marginalized groups.

**3. Collaborative Mathematical Modeling (pp. [S.54]–[S.55])**
Samuel Peterson describes a mathematics lesson focused on disease transmission. Students use an applet and the "5 Practices Model" to create mathematical models that estimate the spread of a virus. The lesson focuses on interpreting parameters in linear or exponential functions—modeled by the function class $F-LE.B.5$—within a real-world, socioeconomic context. The author advocates for interactive and collaborative approaches to make mathematics relevant to understanding contemporary societal issues.

This document outlines two instructional mini-lessons designed for secondary education, focusing on disciplinary and critical literacy.

**1. Housing Insecurity Mini-Lesson (Sands, p. 56–58)**
This lesson uses the Read-Write-Pair-Share strategy to explore housing insecurity through the lens of geometry and social awareness.
* Disciplinary Skill: Applying geometric methods to solve design problems (G-MG.A.3).
* Critical Literacy: Disrupting the commonplace regarding homelessness.
* Methodology: Students read personal accounts (Anthony’s Story or Homeless Essentials), use a Response Heuristics Chart to identify essential items for a tiny house, and engage in peer discussions to refine their design choices based on textual evidence.

**2. Brave New World Character Analysis (Strong, p. 59–61)**
This lesson utilizes a "Character Anatomy" worksheet to help students analyze complex literary characters within the novel *Brave New World*.
* Disciplinary Skill: Analyzing complex characters and their impact on plot and theme (RL.9-10.3).
* Critical Literacy: Interrogating sociopolitical viewpoints and government structures.
* Methodology: Students are divided into four groups, each analyzing a specific character (Bernard Marx, John, Lenina Crowne, or Mustapha Mond). By mapping traits to body parts on an anatomy sheet, groups examine how characters perceive their government and how those perceptions shape their roles within the social hierarchy. Findings are presented to the class to help identify overarching themes and real-world sociopolitical connections.
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: According to the introduction, what is the primary limitation of many traditional approaches to disciplinary literacy instruction?
options: They rely too much on technology and ignore traditional reading and writing strategies.
They fail to provide standardized testing practice for secondary students.
They focus too heavily on student emotional well-being rather than academic standards.
They treat academic knowledge as neutral, static, and disconnected from power structures.
questions.explanation: The text explains that traditional disciplinary literacy often treats academic knowledge as apolitical and static, failing to address the power structures inherent in education.

questions.question: How does Critical Disciplinary Literacy (CDL) differ from conventional disciplinary literacy?
options: CDL ignores disciplinary standards to focus entirely on social justice projects.
CDL challenges students to analyze how power, equity, and dominance shape their specific disciplines.
CDL suggests that literacy skills are the same across all disciplines and do not require specialized instruction.
CDL relies exclusively on student-led lectures rather than teacher-facilitated strategies.
questions.explanation: CDL encourages students to 'name, confront, and analyze' issues of equity and power as they relate to the unique community and discourse of a specific discipline.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the co-authoring process of the textbook?
options: The textbook was written by teacher candidates who scaffolded their learning over a semester with expert feedback.
The textbook was written solely by Dr. Dyches using peer-reviewed research from experts.
The textbook was crowdsourced from anonymous in-service teachers across the country.
The textbook was developed by a national committee of teachers to meet new federal standards.
questions.explanation: The text states the book was co-created by students in EDUC 395 over a 16-week process involving guest speakers, drafts, and peer feedback.

questions.question: In Ridwan Abdi's math lesson, how is critical literacy integrated into the study of residential segregation?
options: Students purely memorize definitions of segregation before solving equations.
Students write a persuasive essay on why math is the most important subject for social justice.
Students use mathematics to model how small individual biases can lead to systemic societal tipping points.
Students perform complex calculus to estimate future population growth in cities.
questions.explanation: Abdi's lesson links math skills (percentages/fractions) with an analysis of how 'smallest of biases' lead to segregation, meeting the critical literacy dimension of focusing on sociopolitical viewpoints.

questions.question: What is the primary goal of the Socratic Seminar strategy in Samantha Andersen’s biology lesson?
options: To encourage students to critically evaluate data and discuss the human impact and equity issues in climate risk.
To have students lecture their peers on the historical causes of global warming.
To ensure that only the most knowledgeable students provide the correct answers to scientific problems.
To test students on their ability to memorize scientific definitions regarding climate change.
questions.explanation: The seminar aims to get students to use critical thinking and problem-solving skills to analyze data and discuss the 'why' and 'who' behind climate change impacts, not just memorize facts.

cards.back: A teaching approach that understands disciplines as unique communities while prompting students to name, analyze, and disrupt power structures, inequities, and oppression within those disciplines.

cards.front: What does CDL add to traditional disciplinary literacy?
cards.back: It shifts the focus from solely 'thinking like an expert' toward analyzing how academic knowledge is connected to power, while explicitly addressing issues of equity and social justice.

cards.front: List the four dimensions of critical literacy defined by Lewison, Flint, and Sluys (2002).
cards.back: 1. Disrupting the commonplace, 2. Interrogating multiple viewpoints, 3. Focusing on sociopolitical viewpoints, 4. Taking action and promoting social justice.

cards.front: Why is it potentially problematic to only teach students to 'think like an expert'?
cards.back: It can implicitly alienate students from marginalized groups by suggesting their own 'funds of knowledge' are incompatible with established academic standards.

cards.front: What main issue does the textbook 'Supporting Secondary Teachers’ Critical Disciplinary Literacies' aim to solve?
cards.back: The lack of resources that effectively merge standard-aligned disciplinary literacy instruction with justice-oriented, critical pedagogy.

cards.front: What does 'reading the word and the world' mean within the context of CDL?
cards.back: It refers to the capacity to analyze both disciplinary texts and the real-world social, political, and power dynamics inherent to those subjects.

cards.front: How are CDL lesson chapters in this text structured?
cards.back: They include disciplinary standards, critical literacy dimensions addressed, necessary resources, step-by-step implementation instructions, a video demonstration link, and a brief author biography.

cards.front: According to the text, why is it vital for pre-service teachers to learn CDL strategies?
cards.back: To prepare them to support secondary students' learning in engaging, sociopolitically relevant ways that promote equity, agency, and justice.

cards.front: In Ridwan Abdi's 'Residential Segregation' lesson, what math skill is paired with the critical literacy objective?
cards.back: Converting fractions and decimals to percentages while analyzing sociopolitical viewpoints on segregation.

cards.front: What is the primary instructional goal of the Socratic Seminar in Samantha Andersen’s Biology lesson?
cards.back: To design, evaluate, and refine solutions for human-driven climate change while interrogating multiple viewpoints regarding environmental risk and responsibility.

slides.title: Lesson 3: Disciplinary Literacy
bullets: Understanding content reading and writing strategies.
Defining Critical Disciplinary Literacy (CDL).
Integrating standards with social justice.
Leveraging power-conscious pedagogical tools.
slides.notes: This lesson introduces critical disciplinary literacy, moving beyond traditional methods to empower students by connecting academic skills to real-world power structures.

slides.title: Supporting Secondary Teachers
bullets: Disciplinary literacy moves beyond "all teachers are reading teachers." (p. 6)
It de-mystifies unique disciplinary thinking for students. (p. 6)
Traditional views often treat disciplines as apolitical. (p. 6)
CDL explicitly connects knowledge to power structures. (p. 6)
slides.notes: Traditional literacy approaches often ignore the power dynamics inherent in academic disciplines; CDL challenges this by centering equity.

slides.title: Residential Segregation & Math
bullets: Skill: Convert fractions and decimals to percent. (p. 11)
Uses Parable of the Polygons to visualize bias. (p. 12)
Students analyze the sociopolitical impact of housing. (p. 12)
Reflects on the math behind societal tipping points. (p. 12)
slides.notes: Math becomes a tool for social analysis by using real-world simulations to understand how minor biases result in large-scale segregation.

slides.title: Biology & Climate Change
bullets: Skill: Refine solutions for human environmental impact. (p. 14)
Seminar focuses on interrogating multiple viewpoints. (p. 14)
Students analyze climate risk data for local connections. (p. 15)
Requires critical thinking and collaborative listening. (p. 16)
slides.notes: Through Socratic seminars, students engage in data-driven discussions that challenge them to solve complex environmental problems from multiple perspectives.

slides.title: Debate & Interpretive Communication
bullets: Interrogating multiple perspectives through language. (p. 18)
Analyze quotes using personal and historical context. (p. 19)
Scaffolded approach: personal opinion to debate. (p. 19)
Students reconcile ideology with historical consequences. (p. 19)
slides.notes: This strategy uses critical discourse to analyze how rhetoric can be weaponized in political movements, encouraging students to trace real-world impacts.

slides.title: Poster Carousel: Poverty Analysis
bullets: Skill: Negotiate meaning in the target language. (p. 21)
Topic: Examining systemic poverty in Latin America. (p. 21)
Students share opinions on sociopolitical issues orally. (p. 22)
Rotate questions to synthesize collective reflections. (p. 22)
slides.notes: Language learners gain proficiency while simultaneously grappling with complex sociopolitical realities affecting the cultures they study.

slides.title: Cornell Notes: Music & Humanity
bullets: Analyze central ideas and textual development. (p. 24)
Focus on elements: rhythm, pitch, harmony, melody, timbre. (p. 25)
Compare/contrast human uniqueness against other species. (p. 25)
Integrates philosophical inquiry into academic note-taking. (p. 24)
slides.notes: Cornell notes are used here as a structured analytical tool, helping students track and refine conceptual arguments during media consumption.

slides.title: RAFT: Pascal’s Triangle
bullets: Interpret complicated mathematical expressions. (p. 27)
Role, Audience, Format, Topic (RAFT) strategy. (p. 27)
Examine credits and marginalized voices in history. (p. 28)
Discuss who deserves historical scientific recognition. (p. 28)
slides.notes: The RAFT strategy disrupts the commonplace by forcing students to re-evaluate the history of mathematical discovery through diverse role-playing.

slides.title: Fishbowl: Gender Inequality
bullets: Propel collaborative conversation and challenge ideas. (p. 30)
Inquiry into sociopolitical viewpoints of gender. (p. 30)
Inner/outer circle format fosters active listening. (p. 31)
Reflections serve as critical exit tickets. (p. 31)
slides.notes: The fishbowl technique centers student voices while providing a structured observation space for evaluating complex debates on inequality.

slides.title: Annotating Text: Social Justice
bullets: Analyze impact of word choices on tone. (p. 32)
Annotate for connections across disparate texts. (p. 33)
Record reactions to antiracist policy concepts. (p. 34)
Develop deeper engagement through margin notes. (p. 33)
slides.notes: Annotation transforms reading into a transformative act, where students document their own critical relationship with the text.

slides.title: The Great Depression Jigsaw
bullets: Examine economic reforms during the depression. (p. 36)
Jigsaw method to synthesize complex historical texts. (p. 36)
Analyze interactions between government and capitalism. (p. 36)
Interrogate multiple perspectives on economic instability. (p. 36)
slides.notes: Using the jigsaw method allows students to become experts on specific facets of historical economic policy before synthesizing them as a group.

slides.title: Minimum Wage Collaborative Maps
bullets: Represent/analyze relationships between independent/dependent variables. (p. 39)
Utilize Jamboard for collaborative wage plotting. (p. 40)
Compare linear growth against cost of living. (p. 40)
Identify socioeconomic inequality through mathematical slopes. (p. 40)
slides.notes: Math becomes a critique of the living wage by modeling how different job earnings impact individual capacity to afford basic living costs.

slides.title: Book Banning Fishbowl
bullets: Discuss censorship and classroom materials. (p. 42)
Focus on sociopolitical viewpoints regarding bans. (p. 42)
Generate questions through pro/con grouping. (p. 42)
Analyze the impact of removing diverse narratives. (p. 42)
slides.notes: A fishbowl discussion on book banning provides a safe forum for students to debate the intersection of literacy, politics, and power.

slides.title: TPCASTT: “War Girls” Analysis
bullets: Analyze figurative language and voice. (p. 46)
Disrupting the commonplace: centering women's history. (p. 46)
TPCASTT + CL format for structured deep reading. (p. 47)
Evaluating themes of war and gender. (p. 48)
slides.notes: TPCASTT adapts to incorporate critical literacy, shifting the focus from simply identifying literary devices to questioning why certain voices are omitted.

slides.title: Housing Insecurity & Tiny Homes
bullets: Use geometric methods for design constraints. (p. 56)
Read-Write-Pair-Share on homelessness and class. (p. 57)
Disrupting commonplace views on individual needs. (p. 57)
Textual evidence supports design justifications. (p. 56)
slides.notes: Students use geometry to design tiny homes, linking mathematical constraints with a critical examination of housing insecurity and social class.

slides.title: Brave New World Anatomy
bullets: Analyze complex character development and motivation. (p. 59)
Apply character traits to "anatomy" visuals. (p. 60)
Interrogate varied government/power perspectives. (p. 60)
Collaborative analysis of thematic character roles. (p. 60)
slides.notes: Using character anatomy charts, students can map out the internal and external conflicts of characters in dystopian fiction to understand societal roles.
